entries.content: When selecting agents for tasks, consider the task type, required expertise, and agent capabilities. Codex excels at code tasks, Scout at research, Cipher at security audits.
Use Q-learning to optimize agent assignment over time. Track success rates, update Q-values based on outcomes, and gradually converge on optimal pairings.
Maintain an organized KB with clear topics, relevant content, and consistent tagging. Regular updates from agent outcomes improve system learning.
For RAG systems: retrieve relevant context first, rank by relevance, inject into prompts with clear markers, monitor retrieval quality metrics.
Score confidence of agent selections using bootstrap methods or uncertainty quantification. Flag low-confidence decisions for manual review or escalation.

Nikola Tesla (1856-1943) was a Serbian-American engineer and inventor. Primary achievement: design of the modern AC (alternating current) electrical system, now the global standard. Other major inventions: induction motor with rotating magnetic field (1888), Tesla coil transformer (1891), wireless power transmission research (1891-1905), remote-controlled boat (1898), and high-frequency lighting experiments (neon/phosphorescent). Tesla held approximately 300 patents worldwide (~111 U.S. patents) across AC systems, motors, wireless transmission, turbine designs, and high-frequency apparatus. Key partners: George Westinghouse (commercialization), Key rivals: Thomas Edison (DC vs AC).

AC Electrical System (1883-1895): Modern alternating current power distribution; patented US390415 + 7 polyphase patents (1888); foundation of global electrical infrastructure. Induction Motor (1883-1888): First practical AC motor using rotating magnetic fields; 7 U.S. patents; enables machinery to run on AC power. Tesla Coil (1891-1897): High-voltage transformer achieving 20,000 cycles/second; patents US568176A, US568179A (1896); still used in radio and wireless technology. Wireless Power Transmission (1891-1905): Long-distance power transmission without wires; patent US649621A (1900); Wardenclyffe Tower on Long Island (partially built, abandoned 1905).

Colorado Springs Laboratory (1899-1900): High-voltage and high-frequency experiments; 60+ technical records documented. Remote Control Technology (1898): Teleautomatic boat demonstrated at Madison Square Garden; early wireless remote control pioneer. X-Ray Research (1891-1895): Shadowgraph experiments parallel to Röntgen's X-ray discovery. Neon & Phosphorescent Lighting (1890s-1900s): High-frequency power led to neon light creation; foundation of modern neon signage. Tesla Turbine (1906-1913): Bladeless rotor turbine design; patents documented. Mechanical Oscillator (1890s-1912): Resonance-based vibrator; some claims legendary, core device documented.

Particle Beam Weapon (1930s-1942): Claimed to accelerate mercury particles at 48x speed of sound; proposed range 250 miles; offered to US (declined) and USSR (tested 1939); declassified CIA documents exist. Thought Photography Device (1920s-1930s): Theoretical concept to project thoughts as screen images; no experimental evidence. Cosmic Ray Motor: Theoretical concept to power motor with cosmic radiation; speculative only. Status: These projects lack experimental verification or successful implementation.

Primary Archives: Nikola Tesla Museum (Belgrade, Serbia) - Original papers, notebooks, correspondence, designs; Library of Congress (Washington DC) - Correspondence 1890-1934 on microfilm; Smithsonian Institution - H.W. Lende Jr. Manuscript Collection; Internet Archive (archive.org) - Free access to complete Tesla Papers. Published Works: 'My Inventions' autobiography (1919, compiled 1983), 'The Fantastic Inventions' (compiled 1993), 'The Inventions, Researches and Writings of Nikola Tesla' (1894), 'Tesla Said' (comprehensive article collection). Free Online: Google Patents (all Tesla patents), Tesla Collection (most comprehensive), Tesla Universe, Tesla Science Center.
